I found a shiny metal cave with lights that scream, but I broke the screaming box with my rock.
The sky-fire fell from the stars and burned the forest, so now I hunt the strange, glowing beasts in the ash.
I do not know why the metal men in the floating city cry, but I will take their soft skins for my winter bed.
The great stone wall of the ancestors is crumbling into the sea, and the spirits of the old world are waking up hungry.
I found a magic mirror that shows a city of gold, but I used it to scrape the fish scales from my dinner.
I smash table with heavy stone because small man in suit talk too much about synergy.
This big paper with many marks is not good for hunt, so I rip it to make fire.
Other tribe want join my tribe, but they must bring many mammoth meats to prove strength.
I not like cold room with glass walls; it feel like ice cave where saber-tooth hide.
I grunt loud at the gold-hair chief to show him that I am the alpha of this merger.
I claim the valley of the Great River because my ancestors carved their marks into the red stone long before your people arrived.
My club will crush the skull of any warrior who dares to steal the fire-embers from our sacred hearth.
We must hunt the mammoth together or we shall both perish in the frost of the coming long-night.
I saw your hunters crossing the forbidden ridge, and now the blood-debt must be paid in skins and flint.
The Great Spirit spoke to me in the cave-echoes, telling me that your tribe must leave this forest or face the wrath of the storm.
The glowing glass paths beneath my feet feel like frozen rivers that never melt, and they hum with a magic I cannot name.
I do not understand why the great metal birds scream across the sky without flapping their wings.
This mountain of steel and light breathes like a living beast, but it has no heart that I can find.
The walls speak to me in voices of silver, telling me things about a world I have never seen.
I try to hunt the shimmering ghosts that dance in the air, but my spear only passes through their light.
I do not understand why these floating ghosts call this place a courtroom when there are no animals to hunt and no fire to keep us warm.
The Great Spirit Judge looks at me with eyes like thunder, but I only used my club because the shimmering spirit-beast tried to steal my favorite rock.
I tried to tell the lawyer-demon that my cave was my home, but he keeps talking about laws written on skins that I cannot read.
My heart beats loud like a drum in this cold hall of marble, and I fear the glowing scales will decide my soul is too heavy with the blood of the mammoth.
I do not know what a "witness" is, but I will tell the truth to the shimmering court: the fire started when the sky-man touched the dry grass.
This metal cave has cold walls that do not breathe, but it keeps the crushing black water away from my skin.
I found a glowing stone that hums in the dark, and I keep it near my bed to frighten the shadows.
The long white tunnels are like the belly of a great dead whale, smelling of old salt and rusted iron.
I hunt the pale, eyeless fish that swim through the broken glass windows when the ocean leaks inside.
There are ghosts of the old people trapped in the humming boxes, speaking in tongues I cannot understand.
I do not understand why this white wall is empty, for there is no bison or handprint here to tell me who lived in this place.
This strange stone floating in the air makes my heart race, as if a great spirit has frozen a boulder in the middle of a jump.
I touch the cold metal sculpture and wonder why the hunters of this tribe spent so much time making a shape that cannot be eaten or used for warmth.
The loud, screaming noises coming from the black boxes frighten me, sounding like a wounded mammoth trapped in a cavern of glass.
I see my own reflection in the polished floor and feel as though I am walking upon a frozen lake that mirrors the sky of a world I do not know.
This stone village smells of rot and death, and I do not like how the air tastes of sickness.
The metal-men in masks tell me to stay away from the bodies, but I only want to bring my kin to the warm fire.
I saw a man’s skin turn black and bubble like boiling mud, so I hit him with my club to stop the evil spirit.
These people cry to a god in a tall stone house, but they should be hunting deer to keep their bellies full while the world dies.
I found a soft bed of furs in a hut where the people stopped breathing, so I took them for my own nest.
I touch the stone wall and my hand slips through the gray rock like it is made of smoke.
The great sun in the sky flickered twice, and for a heartbeat, I saw a ceiling of humming metal lights.
I tried to scream at the saber-tooth, but my voice came out as a series of rhythmic beeps and static.
The river suddenly stopped flowing and froze in mid-air, leaving a thousand shimmering diamonds suspended in the wind.
I stepped across the valley floor and found myself instantly standing back where I started, as if the world had looped.
